request.question: Where was the first store that the grandfather of Elinor S. Gimbel's husband located?

title: Gimbels
Welty's General Store
Media Play
Congress of American Women
Sutherland's Drug Store
Kaufman-Straus
Dunn Bros
Anders Rudolph Rude
Discount Drug Mart
Fred Gottschalk Grocery Store
sentences: Gimbel Brothers (Gimbels) was an American department store corporation from 1887 until 1987.

Adam Gimbel opened the first store in Vincennes, Indiana that would later lead to his son Bernard opening the first store in the chain of Gimbels Department Stores in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.The company is known for creating the Gimbels Thanksgiving Day Parade, the oldest parade in the country.
Gimbels was also once the largest department store chain in the country.
By the time it closed in 1987, Gimbels had 35 stores in Pennsylvania, New York, New Jersey, Wisconsin, and Connecticut.
Welty's General Store was first established in a log cabin just outside the town of Dubois, Wyoming in 1889 by Frank A. Welty.
The store was placed next to the homestead cabin of his father, Dr. Francis Welty.
In 1897 George Hays and Huey Yeomans built a general store in Dubois.
Welty purchased the store in 1898 and moved his operation into town, dismantling the first store and appending it to the new store.
Both buildings were of log construction with an earth roof.
Media Play was a chain of retail stores founded in 1992 by Musicland that sold movies on video, DVDs, music, electronics, video games, books, and games in the United States.
Each store essentially contained a book store, a movie store, a music store, and a video game store under one roof.
At their height, they operated 72 stores in 19 states with 2,000 employees.
The first store opened in Rockford, Illinois, in 1992.
Hundreds of stores were slated to be opened, but only 89 ever were.
The Congress of American Women was an American women's rights organization.
It was founded in New York on March 8 (International Women's Day) 1946 following a 1945 feminist conference in Paris.
Its primary organizer was Elinor S. Gimbel (wife of Louis S. Gimbel, Jr., grandson of Adam Gimbel of Gimbels department store).
It was affiliated with the Soviet sponsored organization Women's International Democratic Federation.
In 1948 the organization was accused of being a communist front organization by the House Un-American Activities Committee and was forced to register as a "subversive" organization.
The organization was finally dissolved in 1950.
Among its members were anthropologist Gene Weltfish, aviator Jacqueline Cochran, educator Charlotte Hawkins Brown, and author and artist Muriel Draper.
Sutherland's Drug Store is a small family-owned drug store in Yellowknife, Northwest Territories, Canada.
The company was established in Fort McMurray, Alberta by Angus Sutherland in 1918 and expanded into the Yellowknife gold fields during the rush in 1938.
Its first store was located in Old Town Yellowknife and was managed by Walter Hill and Keith Miller.
The store closed during the war but reopened and expanded following the post-war gold boom.
In 1948, the Old Town store was physically moved to the new downtown development and a second store was built in Old Town.
Two stores were run by the chain into the 1950s.
In 1951, Doug and Wilma Finlayson and A.L. Blackberg bought the Sutherland chain following the death of Angus Sutherland.
In 1954, the Old Town store closed; in 1956, the current downtown store was built and the original store brought up from Old Town years previous was rented to commercial tenants.
Sutherland's Drug Store continues to operate today in its expanded storefront and successfully competes with larger department stores in Yellowknife.
Kaufman-Straus was a local department store that operated in Louisville, Kentucky, from 1879 to 1969.
In 1879, local retail clerk Henry Kaufman opened the first store on Jefferson between 7th and 8th.
Four years later, Benjamin Straus entered into partnership with Kaufman.
In 1887, the Kaufman-Straus store moved to South 4th Street in space leased from the Polytechnic Society of Kentucky.
The new flagship store opened in 1903, at 533-49 South 4th Street, designed by local architect Mason Maury.
In 1924, Kaufman-Straus was acquired by City Stores Company and the following year the flagship store underwent extensive renovations.
City Stores rebranded the company as Kaufman's in 1960.
It operated two stores in suburban Louisville at The Mall and Dixie Manor.
In 1969, Kaufman's was acquired by L. S. Ayres, and the downtown Louisville store was subsequently closed in 1971.
The flagship store was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1983.
Dunn Brothers Coffee is a franchise company of coffeehouses founded in St. Paul, Minnesota, in December 1987 by brothers Ed and Dan Dunn.

The first store was opened on Grand Avenue in St. Paul, after the brothers had driven an old Dodge van loaded with their belongings from Portland, Oregon, to Minnesota in search of a good, underserved location for a coffee business.
They found a refurbished 1950s Probat roaster in Cincinnati, Ohio, and used it to set up that first store.
The second location opened in Uptown Minneapolis in 1991 (and was temporarily the only location while the Grand Avenue store was rebuilt following a 1991 fire).
Anders Rudolph Rude (October 25, 1812 in Copenhagen – May 21, 1883 in Georgetown, Texas) was a Danish-American Lutheran minister and theologist.
Having graduated from the University of Copenhagen he emigrated to the United States of America in 1836, arriving in Boston.
He settled in Virginia and graduated again from the Gettysburg seminary and was ordained as a minister in Woodstock, Virginia.
He married Anne Steenbergen "née" Caperton, and took over the administration of the plantation of her late husband located at Locusts Grove in Shenandoah County, Virginia.
There he built a church on the hill which was later to take his name, Rude's Hill.
During the American Civil War he lost everything as the plantation came to be used as the headquarters for General Stonewall Jackson in 1862.
He lost his wife and daughter as the Confederate and Union forces struggled over the Shenandoah Valley from 1862 to 1864.
Discount Drug Mart, Inc., is an Ohio-based drug store chain that opened its first store in Elyria, Ohio in 1969.
It now operates 71 stores in more than 20 of Ohio’s 88 counties, with its corporate headquarters and warehouse based in Medina, Ohio.
The Hilliard location recently closed due to lack of profit.
This is the first store out of the chain to close.
The store prides itself on its one-stop shopping and the variety of products it provides, and it says it all in their slogan 'We Save You the Runaround'.
Today, Discount Drug Mart has a revenue of over $550 million and continues to expand, catering to smaller cities that do not have the population to attract a larger retailer.
The Fred Gottschalk Grocery Store is a historic grocery store building located at 301 West Edwards Street in Springfield, Illinois.
Fred Gottschalk opened his first store at the site in 1887; he built the present building in 1898.
The brick building has a Commercial style design with a cast iron storefront and a corbelled cornice.
The store, one of several groceries in the area, served many of Springfield's prominent politicians and their families; it also allowed its customers to purchase goods by telephone.
Gottschalk and his son Arthur ran the store until 1971; the building is now one of the few surviving neighborhood groceries in Springfield.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Where was the first store that the grandfather of Elinor S. Gimbel's husband located?
hop1_docs: Gimbels | Gimbel Brothers (Gimbels) was an American department store corporation from 1887 until 1987.  Adam Gimbel opened the first store in Vincennes, Indiana that would later lead to his son Bernard opening the first store in the chain of Gimbels Department Stores in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.The company is known for creating the Gimbels Thanksgiving Day Parade, the oldest parade in the country.  Gimbels was also once the largest department store chain in the country.  By the time it closed in 1987, Gimbels had 35 stores in Pennsylvania, New York, New Jersey, Wisconsin, and Connecticut.

Congress of American Women | The Congress of American Women was an American women's rights organization.  It was founded in New York on March 8 (International Women's Day) 1946 following a 1945 feminist conference in Paris.  Its primary organizer was Elinor S. Gimbel (wife of Louis S. Gimbel, Jr., grandson of Adam Gimbel of Gimbels department store).  It was affiliated with the Soviet sponsored organization Women's International Democratic Federation.  In 1948 the organization was accused of being a communist front organization by the House Un-American Activities Committee and was forced to register as a "subversive" organization.  The organization was finally dissolved in 1950.  Among its members were anthropologist Gene Weltfish, aviator Jacqueline Cochran, educator Charlotte Hawkins Brown, and author and artist Muriel Draper.

Schuster's | Schuster's, officially Ed.  Schuster & Co., was a department store chain, founded in 1883, in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, now defunct.  The chain opted for neighborhood stores over a downtown location.  Gimbels bought Schuster's in 1962 and operated for a few years as Gimbel Schuster's.

Lit Brothers | Lit Brothers was a moderately-priced department store based in Philadelphia.  Samuel and Jacob Lit opened the first store at North 8th and Market Streets in 1891.  Lit's positioned itself well as a more affordable alternate to competitors Strawbridge and Clothier, Wanamaker's, and Gimbels.  The store's slogan was "A Great Store in A Great City," and it was noted for its millinery department.

Jeanne Bice | Bice was born on July 20, 1939, in Fond du Lac, Wisconsin, where she was also raised.  She later moved to the nearby town of Ripon, Wisconsin as an adult.  Bice and her husband, Arlow "Butch" Bice Jr., had a son and a daughter, Tim and Lee.  She opened her first store, a women's apparel and gift shop called The Silent Woman, with friend Maryanne Diedrich, as a hobby.  Her original store, which was bankrolled by both of their husbands, was located at Ransom and East Fond du Lac Streets in Ripon.

Elinor Darwin | Elinor Mary Darwin (1879–1954) was an Irish born illustrator, engraver and portrait painter.  Her illustrations were included in several of her husband, Bernard Darwin's books for children.

It (1927 film) | "It" is a 1927 silent romantic comedy film that tells the story of a shop girl who sets her sights on the handsome, wealthy boss of the department store where she works.  It is based on a novella by Elinor Glyn that was originally serialized in "Cosmopolitan" magazine.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Elinor S. Gimbel: passage [2] states she is "wife of Louis S. Gimbel, Jr., grandson of Adam Gimbel" (quote from [2]).
- Adam Gimbel / first store: passage [1] states "Adam Gimbel opened the first store in Vincennes, Indiana" (quote from [1]).
- Alternate "first store" note: passage [1] also says Bernard opened the "first store in the chain ... in Milwaukee, Wisconsin" (quote from [1]).

2) Ambiguities:
- Two "first store" candidates appear: Adam Gimbel (first store in Vincennes, Indiana) and Bernard Gimbel (first store in the chain in Milwaukee, Wisconsin) as shown in passage [1].
- A dedicated "Adam Gimbel" page is not present among the provided passages (missing).

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity: "Adam Gimbel" (exactly as in passages).
- Precise missing fact needed: confirmation of the location (city and state) of the first store opened by Adam Gimbel — and disambiguation whether the question instead intends Bernard Gimbel's "first store in the chain" (Milwaukee, Wisconsin).

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "Gimbels" page (lede/infobox and "History"/"Founding" sections) to verify the statement "Adam Gimbel opened the first store in Vincennes, Indiana" and context about Bernard's "first in the chain" (search: Adam Gimbel first store Vincennes Indiana; check lede/History).
- Open an "Adam Gimbel" page (if it exists) and check lede/infobox and "Career/Founding" for the city/state of his first store (search: "Adam Gimbel" lede infobox Vincennes).
- Open "Elinor S. Gimbel" and/or "Louis S. Gimbel, Jr." pages (lede/family) to confirm the husband/grandfather relationship and ensure the question refers to Adam vs. Bernard (search: "Elinor S. Gimbel" spouse Louis S. Gimbel Jr.).
- If ambiguity persists, open "Bernard Gimbel" page to confirm the "first store in the chain" phrasing and Milwaukee location.

Do not provide the final answer yet — retrieve the above pages/sections to confirm which "first store" is intended and to verify the exact location.
response.hop2_query: Adam Gimbel first store Vincennes Indiana
hop2_docs: Gimbels | Gimbel Brothers (Gimbels) was an American department store corporation from 1887 until 1987.  Adam Gimbel opened the first store in Vincennes, Indiana that would later lead to his son Bernard opening the first store in the chain of Gimbels Department Stores in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.The company is known for creating the Gimbels Thanksgiving Day Parade, the oldest parade in the country.  Gimbels was also once the largest department store chain in the country.  By the time it closed in 1987, Gimbels had 35 stores in Pennsylvania, New York, New Jersey, Wisconsin, and Connecticut.
Adam Gimbel | Adam Gimbel (1817-1896) was the founder of the Gimbel Brothers Company.

Congress of American Women | The Congress of American Women was an American women's rights organization.  It was founded in New York on March 8 (International Women's Day) 1946 following a 1945 feminist conference in Paris.  Its primary organizer was Elinor S. Gimbel (wife of Louis S. Gimbel, Jr., grandson of Adam Gimbel of Gimbels department store).  It was affiliated with the Soviet sponsored organization Women's International Democratic Federation.  In 1948 the organization was accused of being a communist front organization by the House Un-American Activities Committee and was forced to register as a "subversive" organization.  The organization was finally dissolved in 1950.  Among its members were anthropologist Gene Weltfish, aviator Jacqueline Cochran, educator Charlotte Hawkins Brown, and author and artist Muriel Draper.

Roman Catholic Diocese of Vincennes, Indiana | The Diocese of Vincennes (in Latin, Vincennapolis), the first Roman Catholic diocese in Indiana, was erected 6 May 1834 by Pope Gregory XVI.  Its initial ecclesiastical jurisdiction encompassed Indiana as well as the eastern third of Illinois.  In 1843 the Diocese of Chicago was erected from the Illinois portion of the diocese, and in 1857 Diocese of Fort Wayne was erected from the northern half of Indiana.  The seat of the episcopal see was transferred from Vincennes, Indiana, to Indianapolis, and on 28 March 1898 it became the Diocese of Indianapolis.  Pope Pius XII elevated the Indianapolis diocese to an archdiocese in 1944, and erected two new Indiana dioceses: the Diocese of Evansville and the Diocese of Lafayette.  The Diocese of Gary, Indiana, was erected in 1956.  The Evansville Diocese absorbed the city of Vincennes upon its creation.

Simon Bruté | Simon William Gabriel Bruté de Rémur (March 20, 1779 – June 26, 1839) was a French missionary in the United States and the first bishop of the Diocese of Vincennes, Indiana.  President John Quincy Adams called Bruté “the most learned man of his day in America.”
Vincennes Bridge Company | The Vincennes Bridge Company, based in Vincennes, Indiana, was a designer and builder of bridges that was "one of Indiana's 'most successful bridge-building firms'".

Adam and Sophie Gimbel Design Library | The Adam and Sophie Gimbel Design Library is the visual arts library of The New School University.  Used primarily by students in the Parsons The New School for Design division, it is located in the Sheila Johnson Design Center, in New York City's Greenwich Village.
hop2_titles: Roman Catholic Diocese of Vincennes, Indiana
Adam and Sophie Gimbel Design Library
response.summary_2: Adam Gimbel opened the first store in Vincennes, Indiana. Adam Gimbel (1817-1896) was the founder of the Gimbel Brothers Company.
